coredata.dc:title: Comparison between terbium-doped carbon dots and terbium-functionalized carbon dots: Characterization, optical properties, and applications in anthrax biomarker detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xiaolin
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Rare earth-carbon dots (RE-CDs) nanomaterials combine the merits of both RE and CDs, which have been the promising candidates in the biochemical sensing field. Owing to the diversity of synthetic methods and precursors, the electronic structure, optical properties and sensing performances of RE-CDs vary greatly. Nevertheless, a comparing study focusing on the synthetic methods of RE-CDs using same precursors has not been reported to date. To facilitate the exploration of the interaction mechanisms for RE–CDs, same precursors (folic acid and terbium nitrate) were used to synthesize RE-CDs via two most common fabrication methodologies (doping and direct coordination) in this study. The morphologies, surface groups, and optical properties of the as-prepared terbium-doped CDs (Tb-CDs) and terbium functionalized CDs (Tb@CDs) were comprehensively compared and analyzed. The anthrax biomarker (dipicolinic acid, DPA) sensing performances of both RE-CDs were also evaluated. Taking the original CDs without terbium as control, the Tb-CDs had higher quantum yield (QY) and longer fluorescence lifetime, and the fluorescence emission peak was red-shifted, suggesting the effective passivation of surface defects. On the contrary, QY and fluorescence lifetime of Tb@CDs decreased, and the emission peak remained unchanged, indicating that interaction between free Tb3+ and CDs led to the less excited electrons, similar to the fluorescence quenching behavior of metal ions on CDs. Further characterization also helped in demonstrating the different interaction mechanisms. Moreover, for the superior performance in DPA detection, Tb-CDs was chosen as the probe for the follow-up application. The Tb-CDs exhibited a sensitive response to DPA in the concentration range of 0.05–8 μM with the detection limit of 44 nM. In addition, it was successfully applied to real samples of running water and lake water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rare earth
Carbon dots
Anthrax biomarker

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of dosage of terbium nitrate in the synthesis methods.
(a) XPS survey spectra of CDs, Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs. (b) The zeta potentials of CDs, Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs.
(a) TEM image Tb-CDs (Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution histogram of Tb-CDs). (b) TEM image of Tb@CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs. (b) Excitation (dotted line) and emission (solid line) spectra of CDs (black line), Tb@CDs (red line) and Tb-CDs (blue line).
The comparison of DPA sensing performances (Inset: the FL spectra of Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs after the addition of DPA (0, 0.05 μM, 0.5 μM, and 5 μM)).

(a) Effect of the addition of DPA on the fluorescence spectra of Tb-CDs (Inset: photograph of Tb-CDs excited under 254 nm UV lamps with different DPA concentration). (b)Plot of fluorescence intensity ratio (F544/F450) of Tb-CDs vs the concentrations of DPA.
Schematic representation for synthesizing Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs.
Analysis results and recovery rates of DPA from real samples.

head.ce:title: Comparison between terbium-doped carbon dots and terbium-functionalized carbon dots: Characterization, optical properties, and applications in anthrax biomarker detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030000, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rare earth-carbon dots (RE-CDs) nanomaterials combine the merits of both RE and CDs, which have been the promising candidates in the biochemical sensing field. Owing to the diversity of synthetic methods and precursors, the electronic structure, optical properties and sensing performances of RE-CDs vary greatly. Nevertheless, a comparing study focusing on the synthetic methods of RE-CDs using same precursors has not been reported to date. To facilitate the exploration of the interaction mechanisms for RE–CDs, same precursors (folic acid and terbium nitrate) were used to synthesize RE-CDs via two most common fabrication methodologies (doping and direct coordination) in this study. The morphologies, surface groups, and optical properties of the as-prepared terbium-doped CDs (Tb-CDs) and terbium functionalized CDs (Tb@CDs) were comprehensively compared and analyzed. The anthrax biomarker (dipicolinic acid, DPA) sensing performances of both RE-CDs were also evaluated. Taking the original CDs without terbium as control, the Tb-CDs had higher quantum yield (QY) and longer fluorescence lifetime, and the fluorescence emission peak was red-shifted, suggesting the effective passivation of surface defects. On the contrary, QY and fluorescence lifetime of Tb@CDs decreased, and the emission peak remained unchanged, indicating that interaction between free Tb3+ and CDs led to the less excited electrons, similar to the fluorescence quenching behavior of metal ions on CDs. Further characterization also helped in demonstrating the different interaction mechanisms. Moreover, for the superior performance in DPA detection, Tb-CDs was chosen as the probe for the follow-up application. The Tb-CDs exhibited a sensitive response to DPA in the concentration range of 0.05–8 μM with the detection limit of 44 nM. In addition, it was successfully applied to real samples of running water and lake water.

ce:section.ce:para: For over decades, rare earth (RE) elements have become vital to a wealth of advanced materials and technologies [1]. Due to their unique 4f orbitals and relatively low toxicity, RE ions have fascinating physicochemical properties, which would make them suitable for the preparation of functional materials [2]. But it is worth noting that the 4f-4f transition of RE ions is forbidden transition, resulting in the low molar extinction coefficient and luminous efficiency, which severely limits the application range of RE ions [3,4]. Thus, RE-based materials have been extensively explored to improve their luminescent properties [5–7], but some inherent shortcomings still need to be further improved, such as poor water-solubility and photobleaching [8]. On the other side, as an emerging nanomaterial in the carbon family, carbon dots (CDs) have aroused crucial consideration for those outstanding physical and chemical properties such as high water-solubility, great biocompatibility, low toxicity and tunable luminescence emission [9,10]. CDs are usually defined as a collection of spherical or quasi-spherical zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles (typically less than 10 nm), containing sp2 hybrid carbon structure as the core and abundant functional groups on the surface [11]. But due to the relatively single preparation methods, the types of effective sensing sites on the surface of unfunctionalized CDs are limited (mostly oxygen-containing groups such as carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups), resulting in poor selectivity and sensitivity. Massive efforts have been carried out to improve the functionality of CDs, and the strategies can be mostly disintegrated into heteroatom doping and surface modification [12]. Heteroatom doping can impact the electronic and surface structures of CDs by replacing a few compositions of the host's surface and/or interior [13–16]. Surface modification can combine with other functional structures through covalent or non-covalent form based on the original structure of the CDs surface. Due to the diversity of precursor types and preparation methods, the structural uncertainty of CDs changes the inherent energy band and electronic transition/transfer properties after doping. But surface modification methods rarely involve changes in the internal structure of CDs [12].

As an emerging kind of functional materials, rare earth-carbon dots (RE-CDs) nanomaterials have shown great promise in an extensive range of applications. RE-CDs nanomaterials have higher luminescence stability and better water-solubility than single RE composites, and also have more specific binding sites than pure CDs, which makes RE-CDs have quickly become a promising functional material in biochemical analysis. Generally, luminescent RE-CDs are fabricated through a simple one-step method (doping) or multi-step method (direct coordination or assistance with ligands). The one-step method is to add RE salt to the precursors to synthesize RE-doped CDs. RE ions have widely been applied in doping CDs due to these strong coordination performance and easy interaction with functional surface groups on CDs [17–20]. In addition to the one-step method, RE-CDs can also be fabricated through the multi-step method. The easiest way was to directly mix RE3+ and CDs [21–23]. The abundant surface functional groups on CD allow flexible and easy binding with RE3+. The one-step doping method was simpler and more cost-effective and had more saturated coordination interactions compared with the simple mixing treatment. But limitations in complex structures and the effect on RE coordination ability should be noted. More interesting, RE-CDs have the merits of simultaneously preserving the luminescence of both RE3+ and CDs, suggesting the potential to construct ratiometric probes [20,24,25]. Compared with the single-signal readout mode, ratiometric probes with two signals can effectively eliminate environmental interference, showing better sensitivity and accuracy of signal responses to the target [26,27]. Although there have been various reports on fabrication methodologies and applications of RE-CDs, different precursors and synthetic methods result in different sizes, surface functional groups and binding mechanisms, which means that it is difficult to use previously reported data for comprehensive analysis and comparison. Therefore, it is very important and necessary to synthesize RE-CDs with the same precursors by different methods in order to understand the binding mechanisms and compare the merits and drawbacks of the methods.

In this paper, terbium doped CDs (Tb-CDs) and terbium functionalized CDs (Tb@CDs) were firstly synthesized from folic acid and terbium nitrate via one-step method and multi-step method (Scheme 1 ). The morphologies, surface groups, zeta potentials, and optical properties of the two RE-CDs were characterized. Besides, as the only one biomarker of Bacillus anthracis spores or anthracis disease, dipicolinic acid (DPA) is urgently to be detected efficiently and selectively [28]. Considering its chemical structure, DPA has the salient binding affinity of RE ions and could be the ligand to greatly improve the luminescence efficiency [29]. Therefore, RE-based probes, especially those involving Eu3+ or Tb3+ have been widely developed to detect DPA [30–32]. In particular, there are some recent reports on the detection of DPA by RE-CDs [20,21,33]. Herein, we also compared the performance of Tb-CDs and Tb@CDs on DPA detection, with a view to comprehensively analyze and compare the properties and applications of the two RE-CDs.

Folic acid (FA, C19H19N7O6), terbium nitrate (Tb (NO3)3 ·6H2O), dipicolinic acid (DPA, C7H5NO4), KBr, and CaCl2 were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). NaHCO3, NaClO, Al (NO3)3, KI, MgCl2, ZnCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, quinine sulfate, and hydrochloric acid obtained from Guangfu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). 2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol (Tris) and NaF were acquired from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). The Tris-HCl buffer was prepared by 0.1 M Tris solution and 0.1 M HCl solution. Ultrapure water purified from the United States Milli-Q purification system was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

CDs: 30 mg FA were added into 30 mL ultrapure water followed by an ultrasonic treatment. The mixture homogeneous solution was then transferred into Teflon autoclave. After 180 °C of hydrothermal reaction for 6 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The light-yellow solution was purified with filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MW: 500). Finally, the CDs aqueous was obtained and maintained at 4 °C for further use.

Tb@CDs: 60 mg Tb (NO3) 3 ·6H2O were dissolved into the above CDs solution (30 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 6 h. The resulting mixture was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MW: 500) for 48 h to remove “free” Tb3+. Finally, the Tb@CDs aqueous was obtained and maintained at 4 °C for further use.

Tb-CDs: 30 mg FA and 60 mg Tb (NO3) 3 ·6H2O were added into 30 mL ultrapure water followed by an ultrasonic treatment. The mixture homogeneous solution was then transferred into Teflon autoclave. After 180 °C of hydrothermal reaction for 6 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The light-pink solution was purified with filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MW: 500). Finally, the Tb-CDs aqueous was obtained and maintained at 4 °C for further use.

For the quantitative determination of DPA, 60 μL of as-prepared Tb-CDs (0.3 mg/mL) and different concentrations of DPA were diluted by Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) in a 5 mL calibrated test tube, and the final volume was 5.0 mL. 5 mins later, the emission spectra of the solutions at 450 nm and 544 nm were tested under 270 nm excitation. Ratiometric measurements (F544/F450) were obtained by taking the average of three replicates.

Generally, the amount of RE salt used in the multi-step method (directly mix RE3+ and CDs) to synthesis RE-CDs was often excessive in order to guarantee the interactions between RE3+ and CDs [22,23]. While the amount of RE salt in the one-step doping method was relatively moderate, not as much as possible [19]. Therefore, out of the economic principle, the amount of terbium nitrate was optimized based on the fluorescence intensity of Tb-CDs (Fig. 1 ). Similar as the synthesis method of control CDs, Tb-CDs 1, Tb-CDs 2, Tb-CDs 3 and Tb-CDs 4 (FA: 30 mg; terbium nitrate: 15, 30, 60, 90 mg) were prepared at 6 h and 180 °C. With the increase of terbium nitrate, the fluorescence intensity also gradually increased. When the dosage of terbium nitrate reached to 60 mg, the maximum fluorescence intensity and the fluorescence quantum yield (73.8%) was achieved. Meanwhile, the color of the Tb-CDs solution changed from yellow to light pink, and the emission peak was red-shifted by 10 nm. But the further increase of terbium nitrate (90 mg) only deepened the pink color of Tb-CDs solution, did not continue to redshift the emission peak. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity decreased. This may be related to the high concentration of the reaction solution. Therefore, in this experiment, the both dosage of terbium nitrate in the synthesis of Tb-CD and Tb@CD was fixed at 60 mg for the convenience of comparison. The Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs were prepared as depicted in Scheme 1.

The structure morphologies, surface function groups as well as the zeta potentials in the two kinds of nanoparticles were characterized by TEM, FTIR, XPS, and nano-particle potential analyzer. XPS elemental analyses revealed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and terbium atoms in the Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs, confirming the successful modification or doping of Tb3+ ions on the CDs. Besides, the atomic contents of terbium in Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs were 0.27% and 0.45%, respectively. And the existing forms of terbium in both Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs were Tb3+ and Tb4+ (Fig.S1). Considering the same amount of precursors in the two synthesis methods, the differences in terbium contents suggest that doping may has a stronger ability to enrich terbium than direct mixing. Further deconvolutions of the individual elements (Fig. 2 ) were done to identify their different states. Comparing to the CDs base line, the red shifting of Tb-CDs was more obvious than that of Tb@CDs. Besides, the new peaks of C1s at 289.0 eV and N1s at 404.0 eV were observed (In Fig. 2b, peak of Tb-CDs). Further Fig. 2c (the peak of N1s) and Fig. 2d (the peak of O1s) also illustrated the interaction between Tb3+ and oxygen-containing groups and nitrogen-containing groups in Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs, due to the decreased peaks area at 531.0 ev and 398.5 ev. These differences indicated that the doping of Tb3+ in this study lead to changes of the pristine CDs to a greater extent as compared with surface functionalization.

In order to confirm these changes of surfaces groups, the FTIR spectra of Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs were analyzed. For the control CDs, the big bulge at 3159 cm−1 indicated the existence of a large number of amino (N–H) or hydroxyl (O–H) groups. The peaks at 1671 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO/CN. The absorption bands at 1401 cm−1, 1125 cm−1, and 618 cm−1 were ascribed to C–N, C–O–C and –NH2 group stretching frequencies, respectively [34]. From Fig. 3 a, it can be clearly observed that Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs still have characteristic peaks similar to CDs. But comparing to the CDs base line, the peaks at around 3159 cm−1, 1677 cm−1, 1115 cm−1 and 618 cm−1 smoothed, even disappeared. These discoveries further revealed that selective coordination of Tb3+ with surface carbonyls (CO) groups and amino (-NH2) groups on CDs. Besides, although the addition of Tb3+ has brought some changes to the surface groups, the one-step doping method could retain the active polar groups on the surface of CDs more than the multi-step functionalization method, providing better water solubility and richer binding potential recognition sites. In order to study their surface groups as well as the electron density, the zeta potentials for Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs were measured. As depicted in Fig. 3b, zeta potential value for the control CDs in water was −15.1 mV, revealing the considerably negative charge. Due to the introduction of Tb3+, zeta potential values for Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs were increased to −1.2 mV and 2.48 mV, which further confirmed that doping induced the greater changes of electronic structures.

TEM image (Fig. 4 a) showed that the Tb-CDs had good dispersity with an average diameter of 1.45 nm, which is smaller than that of the CDs (2.55 nm), as shown in Figure S3.When terbium nitrate was added as one of precursors, due to the electrostatic repulsion of Tb3+, the excessive polymerization of folate molecules was largely suppressed, allowing it to undergo a mild carbonization process to form relatively small Tb-CDs particles [35]. Besides, the Tb-CDs possessed a lattice spacing of 0.201 nm, which is a little bit smaller than that of the CDs (0.203 nm). The graphite crystal planes with this kind of spacing may be attributable to the (102) diffraction planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [36]. On the contrast, as illustrated in Fig. 4b, when free Tb3+ ions were mixing with CDs at room temperature to fabricate Tb@CDs, small aggregates were observed clearly from the TEM image due to the multiple coordination sites of Tb3+ ions.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were examined to confirm the optical properties of Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs. As a control, the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs also can be seen in Fig. 5 . CDs showed two obvious UV absorption peaks at 233 nm, 284 nm, and a weak shoulder peak at 339 nm. The peak at 284 nm was probably due to the π-π* transition of the aromatic carbon ring and the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states. In addition, the other two peaks at 233 nm and 339 nm were mainly ascribed to π-π*transition of CC or CN bond and n-π* transition of CO bond [37,38]. After Tb3+ was introduced into CDs, different absorption behaviors were observed. As shown in Fig. 5, the relative intensities of Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs at 233 nm increased sharply, and there were no longer obvious peaks in this interval. In contrast to the peak of CDs at 284 nm, the corresponding peak of Tb-CDs presented a blue shift of 13 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission peak of Tb-CDs was around 450 nm, with a red shift of 10 nm from the peak of the CDs (440 nm), and a blue shift of 10 nm was observed in the excitation peak of Tb-CDs (270 nm) compared with that of CDs, showing that the doping of Tb3+ brought a greater Stokes shift. But at the same time, newly formed Tb@CDs did not change emission peak position of CDs. More interestingly, the fluorescence quantum yield (QYs) of the prepared CDs, Tb-CDs and Tb@CDs were calculated to be 57.12%, 73.8% and 48.2% by using quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as a standard, the variation of QY values were in according with the corresponding fluorescence lifetime. The average FL lifetime of the prepared CDs, Tb-CDs and Tb@CDs were found to be 7.14 ns, 8.47 ns and 6.37 ns, respectively (Fig.S2). It has been reported that some RE ions (Tb3+, Eu3+, Sm3+ …) are “metallic” ions, which can decrease fluorescence intensity and lifetime of CDs, but not change the emission wavelengths. The possible reason could be the formation of dominant ionic bond or weak covalent bond, which only change the excited electrons of CDs instead of bonding structures [39]. Thus, the combining mechanism of Tb@CDs can be similar to the “metallic” behavior on CDs. On the other side, the Tb-CDs had higher QY, larger fluorescence lifetime, and the fluorescence emission peak was red-shifted. Theoretically speaking, for carbon-based fluorescent materials, the larger fluorescence lifetime can be attributed to the effective interaction with organic or inorganic compounds, which can passivate the surface defects that act as excitation energy traps [40]. In this work, doping with Tb3+ passivated the surface of Tb-CDs through the deep conjugation of Tb3+ ions on CDs, decreased the energy of emission, thus led to the red-shift of the emission wavelengths. The previous characterization results also helped to confirm the different fluorescence mechanisms.

Simple mixing will produce unsaturated coordination centers, which easily led to the nonradiative quenching effect of water molecules. But DPA can substitute the water molecules coordinated with Tb3+ to sensitize the fluorescence, thus providing the possibility for Tb@CDs to detect DPA. On the other side, there is no doubt that doping is a more simple and cost-effective way to synthesis RE-CDs and Tb-CDs is more stable due to the saturated coordination interactions. But considering the complicated structures, the coordination ability of Tb3+ in Tb-CDs needs to be further studied. Fortunately, some other reports showed that the RE ions doped on the surface of CDs still have the ability to coordinate with DPA [20,41,42], which was also proved by the experimental results in Fig. 6 . In fact, through the previous characterization and analysis of optical properties, we found that Tb-CDs showed better particle dispersion, higher QY and more Tb content than Tb@CDs, which means Tb-CDs has more potential to become a sensor. In this study, Tb-CDs performed better in DPA detection because of more Tb content and better particle dispersion. As shown in Fig. 6, the Tb3+ on the surface of Tb@CDs and Tb-CDs still had coordination ability and can be combined with DPA to stimulate RE luminescence. Moreover, Tb-CDs had better sensing sensitivity of DPA than Tb@CDs. Therefore, Tb-CDs was chosen as the probe for DPA detection in this study.

To achieve the best analytical performance for DPA, the effects of different reactive conditions (pH and reactive time) were investigated. Fig. S4 showed that the FL intensity ratios (F54 4/F450) in the presence and absence of DPA were stable when the pH value was lower than 7.0. And then it decreased with the raising of the pH value at 7.0–10.0. Therefore, pH 7.0 Tris buffer was selected in the further experiments. Moreover, the FL intensity ratio (F5 44/F450) remained basically unchanged in 60 min, which indicated that the reaction between Tb-CDs and DPA was completed immediately and remained stable at least 1 h. Therefore, in order to be consistent, the incubation time of 5 min was used for the sensing system.

Under the optimum conditions, the ability of Tb-CDs to detect DPA has been studied. Before adding DPA, the main peak of emission spectrum was at 450 nm, which was dominated by the bands of CDs. With the addition of DPA, the characteristic peaks of Tb3+ (490, 544, 585 and 619 nm) appeared and gradually increased due to the synergistical coordination between Tb3+ ions and carboxyl O and pyridine N of DPA, by which DPA efficiently transferred energy to Tb3+ [43]. The most obvious peak at 544 nm was selected as the characteristic output signal. Consequently, the fluorescence emission at 544 nm was dramatically enhanced with the increasing concentration of DPA, while an unobvious signal change at 450 nm was observed (Fig. 7). The ratio of F544/F450 was linear against DPA concentration within the range from 0.05 μM to 8 μM. Furthermore, a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 44 nM was calculated based on the three times standard deviation of eleven blank signals divided by the slope of the linear calibration curve. What is more, excited under 254 nm UV lamp, the visual color changes (from blue to green) with the increase of DPA concentration could be clearly and directly distinguished by the naked eyes. Besides, the analytical properties of our method were compared with reported DPA detection methods (Table S1). Our method showed relatively good detection performance that could meet the detection requirements. In addition, the preparation method of our probe was simple and economical, suggesting great practicability in actual detection.

The selectivity of the nanoprobe for the detection of DPA was further evaluated by separately adding various possible interfering analytes into the sensing system with or without DPA. As shown in Fig. S4, the interferents had no conspicuous influence on F544/F450, indicating an excellent selectivity toward DPA of this dual-signal sensor. The good selectivity was ascribed to specific binding of Tb3+ to DPA, indicating that the Tb-CDs probe was a promising candidate for DPA measurement. In practical application, we used the standard addition method to detect the levels of DPA in river water and running water samples, which were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane in advance. As shown in Table 1 , the results indicated good recovery (97.41%–111.88%), revealing the high reliability and practicality of this approach.

In summary, two RE-CDs (Tb-CDs and Tb@CDs) were firstly synthesized using same precursors, and the properties and applications of two RE-CDs were comprehensively analyzed and compared. Taking the original CDs without terbium as control, the QY and fluorescence lifetime of Tb-CDs increased, and the fluorescence emission peak was red shifted, which may be attributed to fact that Tb3+ effectively passivated the surface defects of CDs. On the other side, Tb@CDs exhibited shorter fluorescence lifetime and weaker fluorescence intensity. The further characterization revealed interaction between Tb3+ and CDs in Tb@CDs was relatively simple and limited to the surface binding, similar to the fluorescence quenching behavior of metal ions on CDs. Tb-CDs had better particle dispersion and uniformity than Tb@CDs. Besides, due to the relatively better sensing performance of DPA, Tb-CDs was chosen as the probe for DPA detection in this study. The output signal (F544/F450) linearly increased with the increasing of DPA concentration from 0.05 μM to 8 μM with a detection limit as low as 44 nM. In addition, it displayed excellent accuracy and recovery in the detection of spiked real samples.
Xiaolin Tian: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhefeng Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence tuning in carbon dots: surface passivation or/and functionalization, heteroatom doping
